title: Behavioral Anchors

content.question: What existing trigger are you not using as an anchor for a new habit?
sop: List the 3 habits you keep failing to stick to.
For each one, name an existing daily action that already happens like clockwork, pouring coffee, sitting at your desk, closing your laptop.
Attach the new behavior directly after that trigger, same time, same place, every time.
Rate how reliable each trigger is, 1-10 (see calculation, page 4), and track completion for 14 days, if compliance is under 70%, the trigger's too weak.
Once the habit runs without thinking, stack the next one onto it.
questions: What existing trigger are you not using as an anchor for a new habit?
Which habit have you tried and abandoned three times, did it ever have a fixed trigger, or just motivation?
What time of day already runs on autopilot that a new behavior could ride on?
content.equation_example: Trigger reliability (rate 1-10) times Repetition (times performed this week, count 0-7+) equals Consistency score. Trigger 8 times Repetition 6 equals 48. Higher means the habit is becoming automatic; lower means the anchor is unreliable or you're skipping too often.
content.closing_statement: Automatic beats motivated, every single time.

caption:
Principle Nº 166: Behavioral Anchors

Anchor behaviors to triggers.

Ask yourself: What existing trigger are you not using as an anchor for a new habit?
